title: Chapter 13 — Reproducibility and Open Science
objectives: Distinguish reproducibility from replicability and state core open-science principles
Anticipate privacy, documentation, and standards barriers that block reproducible work
Apply documentation, metadata, and pinned dataset versions for research-facing reuse
Explain FAIR principles and data-quality practices that support reproducibility
Describe provenance, licensing, privacy, and ethical constraints on open data sharing
Integrate notebooks, containers, and workflow files into reproducible research pipelines
Use continuous integration and automated documentation to keep analyses checkable
Employ R Markdown, DVC, collaboration platforms, and audit trails for team-scale reproducibility

parts.title: Reproducibility and open science
parts.bridge_in: Chapters 8 and 12 covered documentation, versioning, storage, and operational lineage. This chapter turns those assets toward research-facing reproducibility and open science. This opening part defines reproducibility versus replicability and the core principles of open sharing.

parts.title: Challenges and why standards matter
parts.bridge_in: Definitions alone do not make studies checkable. Privacy limits, missing hyperparameters, and incompatible standards still block replication. This part covers those barriers and why reproducibility standards matter for high-stakes decisions.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:13.1 after definitions and open-science principles.

parts.title: Documentation and metadata
parts.bridge_in: Standards require concrete records. Undocumented methods and unpinned dataset versions break replication even when code is public. This part covers research documentation, repository metadata for discovery, and why dataset versions must be pinned.

parts.title: FAIR data principles
parts.bridge_in: Documentation supports humans; FAIR principles make datasets findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable by people and machines. This part introduces FAIR and shows how indexing, access controls, formats, and community standards put the principles into practice.

parts.title: Data quality and provenance
parts.bridge_in: FAIR sharing without quality and provenance still yields fragile science. This part covers data-quality standards for reproducibility and research-facing provenance that tracks how datasets were created and updated.
parts.split_reason: Continues sec:13.4 quality themes and covers sec:13.5 provenance.

parts.title: Licensing and ethical considerations
parts.bridge_in: Openness is not unlimited. Licenses, privacy, bias, and community control constrain what can be shared. This part covers open-data licensing, balancing openness with privacy, and ethical risks in open science.

parts.title: Reproducible research workflows
parts.bridge_in: Licenses and documentation still leave an execution gap. Reproducible workflows capture ordered steps, notebooks, and containerized environments so others can rerun analyses. This part covers workflow files, Jupyter notebooks, and Docker for fixed tool stacks.

parts.title: Automation and continuous integration
parts.bridge_in: Manual reruns drift. Continuous integration and automated tests catch broken preprocessing, environment mismatches, and model regressions before results are published. This part covers CI checks and automated workflow documentation.

parts.title: R Markdown and DVC tools
parts.bridge_in: CI keeps pipelines green; literate tools and data-versioning systems keep analyses and large artifacts aligned. This part covers R Markdown reports and DVC for tracking training data, weights, and pipeline stages.

parts.title: Collaboration, audits, and outlook
parts.bridge_in: The chapter closes with team and future practice. This final part covers cloud collaboration, audit trails that link retraining to data versions, common pitfalls such as environment drift, and the climate-modeling case study that ties the practices together.
